pattern: Each keypress's duration is just the gap since the previous release, so a single left-to-right scan tracking the best (duration, letter) pair so far finds the answer.
hints: The duration of a keypress only depends on when the previous key was released, so work out each duration with one pass instead of comparing every pair of presses.
Track the best duration seen so far and the letter that produced it, updating whenever a strictly longer duration appears.
When a new duration ties the current best, the tie is broken by choosing the lexicographically larger letter, not by which press came first or last.

edge_inputs.description: tie between two durations resolved by letter
every duration ties; the answer is the largest letter among all of them, not the first or last press
the very first keypress (duration equals its own release time) is the unique longest
one huge gap dwarfs a tiny first duration
same letter repeated with different durations, still just tracks the max

stress.description: random strictly increasing release times and random letters up to the max length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 1000))
    keys = ''.join(rng.choice('abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz') for _ in range(n))
    times = []
    t = 0
    for _ in range(n):
        t += rng.randint(1, 1000)
        times.append(t)
    return [times, keys]

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def slowestKey(self, releaseTimes, keysPressed):
        n = len(releaseTimes)
        entries = []
        prev = 0
        for i in range(n):
            dur = releaseTimes[i] - prev
            entries.append((dur, keysPressed[i]))
            prev = releaseTimes[i]
        entries.sort()
        return entries[-1][1]
